David stands before the assembly not as a man clinging to power but as a man releasing it. He has gathered vast resources for the temple—more gold and silver than can be easily fathomed—yet his opening words reveal something more important than the treasure itself. Solomon is young and inexperienced, the task is great, and the palace is not for man but for the LORD God. This is the crucial frame. David does not present the building project as his legacy or his monument. He presents it as God's house, and in doing so, he teaches the assembled leaders what giving truly means. He gives first from his personal treasures, adding to what he has already provided. Then he asks the question that matters: "Now who is willing to consecrate himself to the LORD today?" The response is immediate and generous. The leaders give willingly, the people rejoice at their leaders' giving, and even David rejoices—not in the amount but in the reality that worship has moved through the hearts of God's people.

What makes this moment penetrating is David's prayer in response. He does not accept credit for what has been gathered. Instead, he recognizes a paradox that cuts to the heart of all genuine worship: everything already belongs to God. The riches, the honor, the power—all of it originates in God's hand. So when the people give to build His house, they are giving Him back what He has already given them. David calls himself and his people "foreigners and strangers" in God's presence, echoing the language of pilgrims passing through. Our days are like a shadow, he says, which is not poetry designed to comfort but truth designed to humble. We possess nothing permanently. We hold everything temporarily. Yet in that very temporary state, we have the privilege of offering worship that pleases the God who tests hearts and delights in uprightness. David has given willingly and with an upright heart, and he has seen his people do the same.

The chapter closes with the passing of the kingdom and the passing of a king. Solomon is anointed before the LORD, the assembly offers sacrifices in abundance, and the transition happens with the consent and allegiance of all Israel. David dies at a ripe old age, full of years, riches, and honor—but the text does not linger on his achievements. It directs us instead to the chronicles, the written records kept by prophets and seers. His reign is over. His work continues, but only because it was God's work from the beginning. The question David asked the assembly still echoes: "Who is willing to consecrate himself to the LORD today?" It is a question each generation must answer. It is not asked once. It is asked whenever God's people stand before the opportunity to give, to serve, to surrender what they hold, and to recognize that everything—everything—comes from His hand and belongs to Him.
